subject: Physics
q: A ball of radius $r$ and density $\rho$ dropped through a viscous liquid of density $\sigma$ and viscosity $\eta$ attains its terminal velocity at time $t$, given by $t=A \rho^{a} r^{b} \eta^{\mathrm{c}} \sigma^{d}$, where $A$ is a constant and $a, b, c$ and $d$ are integers. The value of $\frac{b+c}{a+d}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
question: A ball of radius $r$ and density $\rho$ dropped through a viscous liquid of density $\sigma$ and viscosity $\eta$ attains its terminal velocity at time $t$, given by $t=A \rho^{a} r^{b} \eta^{\mathrm{c}} \sigma^{d}$, where $A$ is a constant and $a, b, c$ and $d$ are integers. The value of $\frac{b+c}{a+d}$ is $\_\_\_\_$.
solution:
Using dimensional analysis on $t = A\rho^a r^b \eta^c \sigma^d$:
the dimensions are [T] = $[M L^{-3}]^a [L]^b [M L^{-1} T^{-1}]^c [M L^{-3}]^d$.
Expanding: [T] = $M^{a+c+d} L^{-3a+b-c-3d} T^{-c}$.
Comparing powers: For T: $1 = -c$, giving $c = -1$.
For M: $0 = a + c + d$, so $a + d = 1$.
For L: $0 = -3a + b - c - 3d = -3a + b + 1 - 3d$, giving $b = 3(a+d) - 1 = 3(1) - 1 = 2$.
Therefore $(b+c)/(a+d) = (2-1)/1 = 1$.

q: Consider the efficiency of Carnot's engine is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfrac><msub><mi>log</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mfrac><mrow><mi>β</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>T</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is temperature, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is Boltzman constant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is angular displacement and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> has the dimensions of length. Then, choose the incorrect option.
question: Consider the efficiency of Carnot's engine is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>η</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfrac><msub><mi>log</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></msub><mfrac><mrow><mi>β</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>T</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants. If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is temperature, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is Boltzman constant, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>θ</mi></math> is angular displacement and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> has the dimensions of length. Then, choose the incorrect option.
options: Dimensions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> is same as that of force.
Dimensions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>α</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></math> is same as that of energy.

Dimensions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>η</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></math> is same as that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></math>
Dimensions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is same as that of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>
solution:
Given that, η=αβsinθlogβxkT. Since expression inside the logarithm is dimensionless

βx=kT

Since the dimensions of kT are equivalent to that of energy,

β=kTx=Ex=ML2T-2L=MLT-2

Therefore, β has dimensions of force.

Since efficiency is a dimensionless ratio,

αβ=M0L0T0

⇒β=1α

Therefore, dimensions of α is not same as that of β. Hence option D is incorrect statement and the answer.

xα=βx=ML2T-2. Therefore, α-1x has dimensions of energy.

subject: Physics
q: From the following, the quantity (constructed from the basic constants of nature), that has the dimensions, as well as correct order of magnitude, vis-a-vis typical atomic size, is:
question: From the following, the quantity (constructed from the basic constants of nature), that has the dimensions, as well as correct order of magnitude, vis-a-vis typical atomic size, is:
solution:
As $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{mc}^2$
also $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{F} \cdot \mathrm{s}=\frac{\mathrm{kq} \cdot \mathrm{q}}{\mathrm{r}^2} \cdot \mathrm{r}$
Therefore dimensionally
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{mc}^2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{q}^2}{\mathrm{r}} \\
& \Rightarrow \mathrm{r}=\frac{\mathrm{e}^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 \mathrm{mc}^2}
\end{aligned}$

Then the dimensions of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> will be
question: An expression for a dimensionless quantity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></mfrac><msub><mi>log</mi><mi>e</mi></msub><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mi>k</mi><mi>T</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>β</mi><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math>; where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math> are constants, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is distance; <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi></math> is Boltzmann constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is the temperature. solution:
Given p=αβlnkTβx

We know that expression inside log is dimensionless.

Therefore, kTβx is dimensionless

⇒β=kTx=ML2T-2K-1×K×L-1=MLT-2

Since p is dimensionless ∴ p=αβ=M0L0T0  ⇒α=β   ⇒α=MLT-2

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : The dimensions of Planck's constant and angular momentum are same.
Statement (II) : In Bohr's model electron revolve around the nucleus only in those orbits for which angular momentum is integral multiple of Planck's constant.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement (I) : The dimensions of Planck's constant and angular momentum are same.
Statement (II) : In Bohr's model electron revolve around the nucleus only in those orbits for which angular momentum is integral multiple of Planck's constant.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{E}=\mathrm{hf} \\ & \mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}=[\mathrm{h}] \times\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right] \\ & {[\mathrm{h}]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]} \\ & \mathrm{L}=[\mathrm{MVR}]=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right] \\ & \mathrm{L}=\frac{\mathrm{nh}}{2 \pi}
\end{aligned}$
L is integral multiple of $\frac{\mathrm{h}}{2 \pi}$

subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.
\(\begin{array}{llll} & \text{List - I} & & \text{List - II} \\ A. & \text{Coefficient of viscosity} & I. & [M L^{-1} T^{-1}] \\ B. & \text{Surface tension} & II. & [M L^{0} T^{-2}] \\ C. & \text{Pressure} & III. & [M L^{-1} T^{-2}] \\ D. & \text{Surface energy} & IV. & [M L^{2} T^{-2}] \end{array}\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II.
\(\begin{array}{llll} & \text{List - I} & & \text{List - II} \\ A. & \text{Coefficient of viscosity} & I. & [M L^{-1} T^{-1}] \\ B. & \text{Surface tension} & II. & [M L^{0} T^{-2}] \\ C. & \text{Pressure} & III. & [M L^{-1} T^{-2}] \\ D. & \text{Surface energy} & IV. & [M L^{2} T^{-2}] \end{array}\)
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
(A) Coefficient of viscosity:
$\eta = \frac{F \cdot dr}{A \cdot dv} = \frac{[MLT^{-2}][L]}{[L^2][LT^{-1}]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-1}]$ → I
(B) Surface tension:
$S = \frac{F}{L} = \frac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L]} = [MT^{-2}] = [ML^0T^{-2}]$ → II
(C) Pressure:
$P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{[MLT^{-2}]}{[L^2]} = [ML^{-1}T^{-2}]$ → III
(D) Surface energy:
$E = S \times A = [MT^{-2}][L^2] = [ML^2T^{-2}]$ → IV
Hence, A-I, B-II, C-III, D-IV.

subject: Physics
q:
Match List - I with List - II.
List - IList - II
A. Boltzmann constantI. $[M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$
B. Stefan's constantII. $[ML^2T^{-1}]$
C. Planck's constantIII. $[ML^2T^{-2}K^{-1}]$
D. Gravitational constantIV. $[ML^0T^{-3}K^{-4}]$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List - I with List - II.
List - IList - II
A. Boltzmann constantI. $[M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$
B. Stefan's constantII. $[ML^2T^{-1}]$
C. Planck's constantIII. $[ML^2T^{-2}K^{-1}]$
D. Gravitational constantIV. $[ML^0T^{-3}K^{-4}]$
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Boltzmann constant $k$: $E = k T \Rightarrow [k] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[K]} = [ML^2T^{-2}K^{-1}]$

Stefan's constant $\sigma$: $\dfrac{E}{At} = \sigma T^4 \Rightarrow [\sigma] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[L^2][T][K^4]} = [ML^0T^{-3}K^{-4}]$

Planck's constant $h$: $E = h\nu \Rightarrow [h] = \dfrac{[ML^2T^{-2}]}{[T^{-1}]} = [ML^2T^{-1}]$

Gravitational constant $G$: $F = \dfrac{Gm_1m_2}{r^2} \Rightarrow [G] = \dfrac{[MLT^{-2}][L^2]}{[M^2]} = [M^{-1}L^3T^{-2}]$

Thus, A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I.

Answer: A-III, B-IV, C-II, D-I

a + b + 2c = –1

–2a + b = –2

a = 1, b = 0, c = –1

Y=F1V0A-1

question: If the capacitance of a nanocapacitor is measured in terms of a unit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math>,  made by combining the electronic charge <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math>, Bohr radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msubsup><mi>a</mi><mn>0</mn><mrow/></msubsup><mo>,</mo></math> Planck's constant <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi></math> and speed of light <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> then
solution:
∵     Capacitance C=Q∆V 

Also, hcλ= hca0= Energy. 

∴     C= Q∆V= Q Q∆V Q

∴     C= Q2Energy= Q2 a0hc

∴     Capacitance= Q2 a0hc

⇒     u= e2 a0hc

subject: Physics
q: If $\epsilon, E$ and $t$ represent the free space permittivity, electric field and time respectively, then the unit of $\frac{\epsilon E}{t}$ will be :
question: If $\epsilon, E$ and $t$ represent the free space permittivity, electric field and time respectively, then the unit of $\frac{\epsilon E}{t}$ will be :
solution:
We need to find the unit of $\frac{\epsilon E}{t}$.
From Maxwell's equations, the displacement current density is given by $J_d = \epsilon \frac{\partial E}{\partial t}$.
The term $\frac{\epsilon E}{t}$ has the same dimensions as displacement current density $J_d$.
Current density $J$ is defined as current per unit area, $J = \frac{I}{A}$.
The SI unit of current $I$ is Ampere (A) and the SI unit of area $A$ is $m^2$.
Therefore, the unit of current density is $A/m^2$.
Alternatively, using dimensions:
$[\epsilon] = M^{-1}L^{-3}T^4A^2$
$[E] = MLT^{-3}A^{-1}$
$[t] = T$
Dimension of $\frac{\epsilon E}{t} = \frac{(M^{-1}L^{-3}T^4A^2)(MLT^{-3}A^{-1})}{T} = L^{-2}A$.
This corresponds to the unit $A/m^2$.

q:
Match List I with List II

List - I List - II
A Surface tension I. kg m-1 s-1
B Pressure II. kg m s-1
C Viscosity III. kg m-1 s-2
D Impulse IV. kg s-2

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
Match List I with List II

List - I List - II
A Surface tension I. kg m-1 s-1
B Pressure II. kg m s-1
C Viscosity III. kg m-1 s-2
D Impulse IV. kg s-2

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
(A) Surface tension is related to force and length as  S=Fl=MLT-2 L=M T-2

=kg s-2 (IV)

(B) Pressure is related to force and area as P=FA

 =MLT-2 L2=kg m-1 s-2  (III)

(C) Viscosity in terms of force, area and velocity gradient is given by  η=FAdvdx=MLT-2 L2LT-1L

=ML-1 T-1=kg m-1 s-1 (I)

(D) Impulse is related to force and time of application as J=∫F dt=MLT-2×T

=MLT-1=kg m s-1 (II).

So A-(IV), B-(III), C-(I), D-(II)

q: The speed of a wave produced in water is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>ν</mi><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>λ</mi><mi>a</mi></msup><msup><mi>g</mi><mi>b</mi></msup><msup><mi>ρ</mi><mi>c</mi></msup></math>. The values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>b</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>c</mi></math> respectively, are

solution:
The dimensional form of velocity can be written as

v=λagbρc

⇒M0LT-1=LaLT-2bML-3c

Comparing the exponents for M,L,T,

For M, Mc=M0For L,La+b-3c=L1For T,T-2b=T-1

So,

c=0 ...(i)a+b-3c=1   ...(ii)-2b=-1 ...(iii)

From equations (i),(ii),(iii),

a=12, b=12,c=0

subject: Physics
q: $L$, $C$ and $R$ represents physical quantities inductance, capacitance and resistance respectively. The dimensional formula $ML^2 T^{-4} A^{-2}$ corresponds to __________.
question: $L$, $C$ and $R$ represents physical quantities inductance, capacitance and resistance respectively. The dimensional formula $ML^2 T^{-4} A^{-2}$ corresponds to __________.
solution:
Dimensional formula of inductance $L$ is obtained from $E = \dfrac{1}{2}LI^2$:

$[L] = \dfrac{[E]}{[I]^2} = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-2}}{A^2} = ML^2T^{-2}A^{-2}$

Dimensional formula of capacitance $C$ is obtained from $E = \dfrac{1}{2}\dfrac{Q^2}{C}$:

$[C] = \dfrac{[Q]^2}{[E]} = \dfrac{A^2T^2}{ML^2T^{-2}} = M^{-1}L^{-2}T^4A^2$

Dimensional formula of resistance $R$ is obtained from $P = I^2R$:

$[R] = \dfrac{[P]}{[I]^2} = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-3}}{A^2} = ML^2T^{-3}A^{-2}$

The dimensional formula of $\sqrt{LC}$ is:

$[\sqrt{LC}] = \sqrt{(ML^2T^{-2}A^{-2})(M^{-1}L^{-2}T^4A^2)} = \sqrt{T^2} = T$

Evaluating the dimensional formula of $\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{LC}}$:

$\left[\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{LC}}\right] = \dfrac{ML^2T^{-3}A^{-2}}{T} = ML^2T^{-4}A^{-2}$

This matches the given dimensional formula.

Answer: $\dfrac{R}{\sqrt{LC}}$

solution:
M∝TxCyhz
M1L0T0=T1xL1T-1yM1L2T-1z
[M1 L0T0]=[Mz Ly+2zTx-y-z]]
⟹z=1
y+2z=0 , x-y-z=0
solving
y=-2
and x=-1
M⇒ T-1 C-2 h1

q: In van dar Wall equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>P</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>V</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi><mi>T</mi><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi>P</mi></math> is pressure, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is volume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is universal gas constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is temperature. The ratio of constants <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> is dimensionally equal to :
question: In van dar Wall equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>P</mi><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mi>a</mi><msup><mi>V</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mi>V</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi><mi>T</mi><mo>;</mo><mo> </mo><mi>P</mi></math> is pressure, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi></math> is volume, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi></math> is universal gas constant and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>T</mi></math> is temperature. The ratio of constants <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>a</mi><mi>b</mi></mfrac></math> is dimensionally equal to :
solution:
P+aV2V-b=nRT

Dimensions of b should be equal to dimensions of V. Similarly, dimensions of a will be equal to dimensions of PV2.

Therefore, dimensions of ab=PV2V=PV.

kT  has dimension of energy

βx2kT is dimensionless

βL2=ML2T-2

β=MT-2

α2β has dimensions of work

α2MT-2=ML2T-2

α=M0LT0
